title: Who Told Me Investing Wasn't for Women Like Me?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for tracing the roots of the exclusion belief around investing
excerpt: A journal set for locating the source of the investing-isn't-for-me belief. Works through who taught it, what the household relationship with investment was, and what would need to be true for investing to feel accessible.

practice.whenToUse: Use when investing feels like something for a different demographic and you want to locate where that belief came from.

content.text: A journal set for tracing the investing-isn't-for-me belief back to its source. Most exclusion beliefs about money were absorbed from households where certain kinds of money behaviour were normal and others were not modelled at all.
What is my current relationship with investing?
Do you have a pension? A stocks-and-shares ISA? Anything? Or has investing been something you've thought about and not started?
What does the word 'investor' bring up for me?
Who do you picture? Does that person look like you?
Did anyone in my household invest when I was growing up?
If yes, what did you hear about it? If not, what did that absence communicate?
Who specifically told me (or showed me) that this wasn't for people like me?
It might not have been words. It might have been the absence of a conversation, the people in the adverts, the assumption that financial decisions were handled by someone else.
What would need to be true for me to consider myself someone who invests?
Write the full list without editing. Then look at each condition and mark the ones that are beliefs, not facts.
